decision_scenario: Lo! Foods is expanding into your Tier 2 city. You have the option to try one product from their range: Lo! Keto Cookies (₹120), Protein Chef Bread (₹99), or DiabeSmart Biscuits (₹180). Which would you try first — and what price, claim, or channel would make you a regular buyer rather than a one-time trial customer?

rounds.stimulus: You're in Jaipur (or Lucknow, or Coimbatore). A new health food brand called Lo! Foods has just arrived in your city's modern trade stores — Big Bazaar and a few premium kirana shops. The shelf shows: Lo! Keto Almond Cookies (₹120), Protein Chef Bread (₹99), DiabeSmart Biscuits (₹180). You've heard of 'keto diet' vaguely — maybe from a cousin in Bangalore or from Instagram — but you've never tried keto products. Diabetes is common in your family. What do you pick up to examine, and what do you think the brand stands for?

A local nutritionist in your city has started recommending 'protein-rich breakfast options' on her popular YouTube channel (280,000 subscribers, mostly Tier 2 India). She holds up Protein Chef bread and says: 'Isko try karo — regular bread se teen guna zyada protein hai, aur maida bilkul nahi.' (Try this — three times more protein than regular bread, and absolutely no maida.) She doesn't mention Lo! Foods or keto — just the protein benefit. The video has 45,000 views. Does this change your interest in trying Protein Chef?

Protein Chef bread is now available on Blinkit in your city at ₹99. Your regular bread (local bakery or Britannia) costs ₹40-50. A colleague at your office says he's been eating Protein Chef for a month and feels more energetic at work. He's not a gym-goer — just a regular 35-year-old office worker. At ₹99 vs ₹45, and with a local peer endorsement rather than a fitness influencer, does the protein story resonate enough for you to switch or trial?
rounds.decision: I'm buying the Protein Chef Bread at ₹99 — one loaf, just one, as a trial. It fits into something I already do: buy bread. The price is uncomfortable but not shocking. The channel reassures me that it's not some fly-by-night product. And peer evidence from Rajesh, even if just one person, carries real weight with me. I will not commit to buying it weekly until I've tested it myself and checked the nutrition label carefully. I want to see the protein numbers and compare them to what a boiled egg costs. If my family actually notices they feel less hungry or more energetic, and if the price doesn't change, I'd consider making it a regular purchase. For the DiabeSmart Biscuits — I want to try them eventually, but I need to see WhatsApp reviews, ideally from someone managing pre-diabetes or a family history similar to mine. If Lo! Foods offered a small trial pack of DiabeSmart at ₹60-70 for a smaller portion, I would buy that immediately alongside the bread. As for becoming a regular buyer of any Lo! Foods product: I need consistent stocking in my kirana shop, no sudden price hikes, and honest before/after testimonials from people who look like me — not influencers.
rounds.reasoning: 1. GUT REACTION: My eyes go straight to the DiabeSmart Biscuits — diabetes runs in my family and that fear is always quietly sitting in the back of my mind. But ₹180 for biscuits? I physically put it

rounds.decision: I'm going with the Protein Chef Bread at ₹99 for my first trial. It's the most justifiable choice — it replaces something I already buy, the price is high but not shocking, and I have a real peer reference in Rajesh. I'll buy one loaf, use it for my daughter's school sandwiches and my own breakfast for a week, and honestly assess whether my energy feels different or whether my family notices anything. To become a regular buyer — not just a one-time trial customer — Lo! Foods would need to: price it at ₹79-85 for a 'regular buyer' loyalty tier or subscription, get it stocked at my neighbourhood kirana so I don't have to make a special trip, have consistent positive word-of-mouth from the WhatsApp mothers' group, and ideally run one diabetic-health story alongside the protein story so it connects to my deeper family anxiety. The DiabeSmart Biscuits remain in the back of my mind — if they ever run a trial pack at ₹80-90 for half the quantity, I'd pick it up immediately. That's the product that speaks to my real fear. But I won't pay ₹180 to find out if it actually works.
rounds.reasoning: 1. GUT REACTION: My hand goes straight to the DiabeSmart Biscuits — I can't help it. Diabetes runs in my family, my mother struggles with it every day, and that name feels like it was written for me.

rounds.decision:
I pick up the DiabeSmart Biscuits first — ₹180, one pack, one trial. The diabetes risk in my family makes this the most personally urgent product. But I'm doing this with conditions: I'm reading the label carefully before I pay, looking for glycemic index information, ingredient transparency, and any certification markings. I'll also ask the store staff — or check their website — if any certified diabetologist or clinical nutritionist has reviewed the product. This is a one-time trial purchase.

To become a regular buyer, here's exactly what Lo! Foods would need to do: (1) Make a diabetologist or FSSAI-endorsed nutritionist's recommendation visible and verifiable — not just printed claims, but a name I can look up. (2) Drop the price to ₹130-140 for regular buyers, or offer a subscription/loyalty program — ₹180 every week is not sustainable for my household. (3) Show me a glycemic index comparison vs. regular biscuits — specific numbers. If the GI is genuinely significantly lower and a credentialed expert backs it, I will switch from regular biscuits completely. That's my honest answer.

rounds.decision: I will try the Protein Chef Bread at ₹99 for my first purchase — not DiabeSmart, even though my heart pulled me there. Here is my reasoning: ₹99 is painful but survivable as a one-time trial. ₹180 is too steep without any doctor's recommendation or community word-of-mouth to justify it yet. The local nutritionist on YouTube mentioned the protein claim specifically, and that expert signal — imperfect as it is — tips the scale. I will buy one pack of Protein Chef Bread, compare it honestly to my regular bread over one week, and watch whether my family actually feels different. If the bread tastes decent and my family accepts it, I will ask my family doctor at the next visit whether the DiabeSmart Biscuits are worth considering for my husband's borderline sugar. That medical validation is what would convert me to DiabeSmart. To become a regular buyer of any of these products, Lo! Foods would need to: price Protein Chef Bread at ₹75-80, get a local government hospital dietitian or credentialed diabetes association to endorse DiabeSmart, and make the product available at my local medical store or kirana — not just at a modern trade outlet. A sachets trial pack at ₹30-35 would also remove the risk entirely and get me in the door.
